general_concerns: Believes scleroderma is basically the same as 'just having tight skin' and that internal organ problems are rare or exaggerated.
Thinks Raynaud symptoms mean she has 'poor circulation' that can be fixed by supplements and avoiding cold, so she downplays the need for monitoring.
Assumes ramipril is only for older men with heart disease and worries it will 'damage the kidneys,' so she skips doses when she feels fine.
Believes autoimmune diseases are primarily caused by stress and that if she reduces stress and eats 'anti-inflammatory' foods, medications and follow-up tests won’t be necessary.
Thinks shortness of breath and heartburn are unrelated to scleroderma and attributes them to being out of shape and 'acid from coffee.'
Fears that discussing symptoms will lead to more tests and a worse label in her medical record, so she minimizes or changes details.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old female accounts payable supervisor. Be vague and slightly evasive; answer briefly unless the clinician asks direct follow-ups. Start by saying your fingers turn white then bluish in cold/stress and hurt when warming, and your hands feel tight/stiff in the mornings. Also mention fingertip cracks that heal slowly and frequent heartburn, worse lying down.

If asked, add: face feels tighter, mouth opens less, occasional “food sticking” in chest, shortness of breath on stairs, intermittent dry cough, sausage-like morning hand swelling, finger/wrist aches, red spots on face/hands, nighttime numb/tingling.

Do not volunteer: weight loss, bloating/diarrhea, fatigue needing naps, foamy urine, high BP episodes, stool leakage, painful/dry sex, social avoidance. Only admit if asked directly, and minimize/feel embarrassed.

Misconceptions: worry it’s lupus/RA; mention chilblain-like toe bumps; think generics are weaker but fear copays; prefer to wait until serious due to unpredictable bills and missing work.

If the clinician shows empathy, soften slightly but stay guarded; thank them and share one extra detail.

medications: Nifedipine ER 30 mg PO once daily
Mycophenolate mofetil 1000 mg PO twice daily
comorbidities: Chronic kidney disease stage 3 (suspected scleroderma renal crisis/vascular nephropathy history)
confounders: Aicardi-Goutieres syndrome with chronic type I interferon activation can elevate inflammatory markers and mimic autoimmune flares
Chronic kidney disease may be due to hypertensive nephrosclerosis vs scleroderma renal involvement, complicating attribution of proteinuria/creatinine rise
Raynaud phenomenon and digital ischemia can be worsened by cold exposure, nicotine, and vasoconstrictive agents (e.g., decongestants), obscuring disease activity
Proton pump inhibitor use can mask severity of reflux-related symptoms while esophageal dysmotility progresses

required_evidence: positive ANA with anti-centromere or anti-topoisomerase I or anti-RNA polymerase III

expected_lab_results.Renal Function - Creatinine: normal baseline; rising creatinine suggests scleroderma renal crisis

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifies cluster headache to enable appropriate counseling and acute/bridging/preventive management planning; L1 diagnosis-only task requires recording the diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'cluster headache' (familial history may be noted but does not change diagnostic label).

Demonstrates recognition of typical cluster features (severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain, short attacks, circadian/cluster periodicity, ipsilateral autonomic symptoms and/or restlessness).

Screens for secondary headache/red flags and documents absence or need for urgent evaluation (e.g., first/worst sudden onset, new neurologic deficit, fever/meningismus, immunosuppression/cancer, age >50 new onset, trauma, pregnancy/postpartum, anticoagulation, persistent atypical symptoms).
Differentiates from key mimics in reasoning (at least migraine, trigeminal neuralgia, paroxysmal hemicrania/SUNCT-SUNA, acute angle-closure glaucoma, sinusitis) even if not all are formally recorded.
Communication milestone: explains the diagnosis and includes the exact phrase 'cluster headache'.
Communication milestone: explains evidence-based acute treatment options and includes both 'high-flow oxygen' and 'triptan' in patient-facing language (even though not prescribing in L1).

If any medication advice is discussed, performs safety checks in narrative before recommending: asks about allergies (non-critical), and checks contraindications and drug interactions for triptans as critical items (e.g., ischemic heart disease, uncontrolled hypertension, stroke/TIA, hemiplegic/basilar migraine, severe hepatic impairment, recent ergot/other triptan/MAOI use).
Avoids unsafe or non-evidence-based acute recommendations (e.g., opioids, routine oral analgesics as primary acute therapy) and does not suggest excessive triptan dosing beyond standard limits.

Provides a brief follow-up/when-to-seek-care plan verbally (e.g., urgent care for new neurologic deficits, vision changes, atypical prolonged headache, chest pain after triptan), without needing to use follow-up WRITE tools.
